callouts.label: Pili
callouts.hint: Short straight brown filaments projecting from the top surface of the rod-shaped cell.
callouts.description: Short, hair-like protein appendages on the bacterial surface used for adhesion to host cells, surfaces, or other bacteria. Type IV pili also enable twitching motility. Conjugative pili (F-pili) mediate horizontal gene transfer between bacteria — a process modeled as edges in gene transfer networks.
callouts.ap_tip: Pili are key virulence factors. In bioinformatics, pilus genes are searched in pathogen genomes to predict adhesion capability and pathogenicity.

callouts.label: Ribosomes (prokaryote)
callouts.hint: Clusters of tiny teal dots scattered in the upper area of the rod-shaped cell's interior.
callouts.description: Molecular machines composed of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and proteins that translate mRNA into polypeptide chains. Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S (composed of 30S and 50S subunits), smaller than eukaryotic 80S ribosomes. Both cell types share this essential molecular machine.
callouts.ap_tip: The 70S vs 80S difference is medically important — many antibiotics (e.g., erythromycin, tetracycline) specifically target 70S ribosomes, killing bacteria without affecting human ribosomes.

callouts.label: Cell wall
callouts.hint: Thick gray outermost boundary layer of the rod-shaped cell on the left.
callouts.description: A rigid outer layer composed of peptidoglycan that provides structural support and protection against osmotic lysis. Found in nearly all bacteria. The cell wall is absent in eukaryotic animal cells (though plant and fungal cells have chemically different walls).
callouts.ap_tip: Antibiotics like penicillin target peptidoglycan synthesis — this is why they kill bacteria without harming human cells.

callouts.label: Plasma membrane (prokaryote)
callouts.hint: Orange inner boundary line just inside the gray cell wall of the rod-shaped cell.
callouts.description: A phospholipid bilayer that controls the transport of molecules into and out of the cell. In prokaryotes, the plasma membrane also hosts the electron transport chain and ATP synthesis — functions that eukaryotes delegate to mitochondria. Shared by both cell types.
callouts.ap_tip: Prokaryotic plasma membranes lack cholesterol but may contain hopanoids, which serve a similar stabilizing role.

callouts.label: Nucleoid region
callouts.hint: Tangled purple mass of fibers in the center of the rod-shaped cell, with no surrounding membrane.
callouts.description: The region of the prokaryotic cytoplasm where the circular chromosome is concentrated. Unlike the eukaryotic nucleus, the nucleoid has no membrane envelope — DNA is in direct contact with the cytoplasm. This means transcription and translation can occur simultaneously in prokaryotes.
callouts.ap_tip: The lack of a nuclear membrane is the defining structural difference between prokaryotes and eukaryotes. It directly affects gene regulation: prokaryotes can couple transcription and translation, while eukaryotes cannot.

callouts.label: Plasmid
callouts.hint: Small magenta circular ring in the lower portion of the rod-shaped cell, separate from the main DNA mass.
callouts.description: A small, circular, double-stranded DNA molecule that replicates independently of the chromosome. Plasmids often carry genes for antibiotic resistance, virulence factors, or metabolic capabilities. They are key vehicles for horizontal gene transfer in bacteria and essential tools in molecular cloning.
callouts.ap_tip: In bioinformatics, plasmid sequences must be assembled separately from chromosomal DNA. Many genome assemblers now output both chromosomal and plasmid contigs.

callouts.label: Flagellum
callouts.hint: Long wavy dark teal filament extending from the right end of the rod-shaped cell.
callouts.description: A helical protein filament made of flagellin that rotates like a propeller to propel the bacterium through liquid environments. Powered by a proton motive force motor embedded in the cell envelope. Bacterial flagella are structurally unrelated to eukaryotic flagella/cilia.
callouts.ap_tip: Flagellar genes are often used as phylogenetic markers in microbial ecology studies. The flagellar motor is a classic example studied in molecular evolution.

callouts.label: Plasma membrane (eukaryote)
callouts.hint: Orange outer boundary of the circular cell on the right side of the image.
callouts.description: A phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins, cholesterol, and glycolipids that forms the outer boundary of the eukaryotic cell. Controls molecular transport, cell signaling, and cell-cell recognition. Functionally similar to the prokaryotic plasma membrane but structurally more complex.
callouts.ap_tip: Membrane proteins are major drug targets. In bioinformatics, transmembrane topology prediction (e.g., TMHMM) is a common sequence analysis task.

callouts.label: Nucleus
callouts.hint: Large purple circular structure with a double-line boundary near the center of the circular cell.
callouts.description: The membrane-bound organelle that houses the cell's chromosomal DNA. Enclosed by a double-membrane nuclear envelope perforated with nuclear pores that regulate molecular traffic between nucleus and cytoplasm. Contains the genome organized into chromatin, which condenses into visible chromosomes during cell division.
callouts.ap_tip: The nuclear envelope separates transcription (nucleus) from translation (cytoplasm) in eukaryotes — this spatial separation enables post-transcriptional regulation like mRNA splicing, which prokaryotes lack.

callouts.label: Nucleolus
callouts.hint: Small, very dark purple dense sphere inside the larger purple nucleus.
callouts.description: A dense, non-membrane-bound substructure within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is transcribed and ribosome subunits are partially assembled. The nucleolus is the most active site of RNA transcription in the cell.
callouts.ap_tip: Nucleolar size correlates with ribosome production rate and cell growth — cancer cells often have enlarged nucleoli, which is used as a diagnostic marker.

callouts.label: Rough endoplasmic reticulum
callouts.hint: Blue folded membrane network studded with tiny teal dots, adjacent to the nucleus on its right side.
callouts.description: A network of membrane-enclosed sacs (cisternae) studded with ribosomes on the cytoplasmic face. Synthesizes proteins destined for secretion, membrane insertion, or delivery to other organelles. Newly synthesized proteins enter the ER lumen where they are folded and undergo quality control.
callouts.ap_tip: Protein folding in the ER is relevant to structural bioinformatics — misfolded proteins trigger the unfolded protein response (UPR), and ER stress is implicated in many diseases.

callouts.label: Golgi apparatus
callouts.hint: Stack of curved golden-yellow sacs with small vesicles budding from the edges, below and to the right of the nucleus.
callouts.description: A stack of flattened membrane-bound cisternae that receives proteins from the ER, modifies them (glycosylation, phosphorylation, sorting), and packages them into vesicles for delivery to their final destinations — the plasma membrane, lysosomes, or secretion outside the cell.
callouts.ap_tip: Post-translational modifications added in the Golgi (especially glycosylation) are important in proteomics data analysis — mass spectrometry must account for these modifications when identifying proteins.

callouts.label: Ribosomes (eukaryote)
callouts.hint: Tiny teal dots scattered freely in the cytoplasm of the circular cell, not attached to membranes.
callouts.description: Eukaryotic ribosomes (80S, composed of 40S and 60S subunits) are larger than prokaryotic ribosomes (70S). Free ribosomes in the cytoplasm synthesize proteins that function within the cytoplasm or nucleus. The ribosome is the shared molecular machine across all domains of life.
callouts.ap_tip: Ribosomal RNA sequences are the basis of the universal tree of life — 16S rRNA (prokaryotes) and 18S rRNA (eukaryotes) are the standard phylogenetic markers in microbial ecology.

callouts.label: Cytoplasm
callouts.hint: Light cream-colored interior space filling the area between organelles in both cells.
callouts.description: The gel-like aqueous solution (cytosol) that fills the interior of the cell, along with all suspended organelles, cytoskeletal filaments, and dissolved molecules. In both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, the cytoplasm is the site of many metabolic reactions including glycolysis. Eukaryotic cytoplasm is compartmentalized by organelle membranes; prokaryotic cytoplasm is not.
callouts.ap_tip: Metabolic pathway modeling (Chapter 12) assumes reactions occur in specific compartments — cytoplasm, mitochondrial matrix, ER lumen. Compartment assignment is a key step in building genome-scale metabolic models.

callouts.label: Mitochondrion
callouts.hint: Red-orange bean-shaped organelle with internal wavy folds, in the lower-left area of the circular cell.
callouts.description: A double-membrane organelle that generates most of the cell's ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. The inner membrane is folded into cristae to increase surface area for the electron transport chain. Mitochondria have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes — evidence of their endosymbiotic origin from an ancestral alpha-proteobacterium.
callouts.ap_tip: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) is widely used in phylogenetics due to its maternal inheritance and high mutation rate. Mitochondrial genome assembly is a distinct bioinformatics task from nuclear genome assembly.
